summary: Gruber argues that Siri has improved dramatically in speed and responsiveness, to the point where it can match Google Voice Search in head-to-head comparisons on simple queries. He acknowledges Google may still be better at complex queries but emphasizes that Siri's latency improvements are real and significant. He connects this to Apple's pattern of steady iterative improvement and notes that Siri's poor debut created a lasting negative perception that many users never revisited. He concludes that these improvements are essential for Apple Watch's success, where voice interaction will be a primary input method.
key_points: Siri has become noticeably faster at parsing input and returning results, matching Google Voice Search on simple queries in side-by-side testing
Siri debuted on the wrong side of the 'good enough to engender affection' line, creating a negative first impression that proved harder to overcome than building positive perception from scratch
The old conventional wisdom that Google was getting better at design faster than Apple was getting better at web services no longer feels true
Many users gave up on Siri years ago and never revisited it, illustrating the lasting damage of poor first impressions
Siri improvements are essential to Apple Watch success, where voice interaction must work well from launch to avoid a damaging reputation
money_quotes: I want to know whether it's going to be just plain cold, or really fucking cold.
Apple has clearly gotten much better at something everyone agreed was a serious weakness.
Two years later, I don't think 'Google is getting better at design faster than Apple is getting better at web services' feels true any more.
No product or feature is ever perfect when it debuts. Quite the opposite, brand-new products/features usually debut needing numerous obvious improvements.
Ideally, they should debut on the right side of the 'good enough to engender affection' line.
Siri, I think it's clear in hindsight, debuted on the wrong side of that line.
It's harder to change a negative perception than it is to create a positive one from a blank slate.
Apple will find itself in a deep hole if voice dictation via Apple Watch gets saddled with an 'Egg Freckles'/'Eat up Martha' reputation.
key_insight: Siri's steady, unglamorous improvements have closed the gap with Google Voice Search, but the lasting damage of its poor debut shows that first impressions matter more than iterative improvement in shaping user perception.
